title: 🟡 Intervals — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Sort by start time to merge overlapping ranges efficiently.
description: Master the Interval pattern. Always sort first, then compare `current.end` with `next.start`.

roi.complexity: Time: O(N log N) | Space: O(N)

codeLines.text: // STEP 1 — SORT: Sort intervals by start time
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Iterate through the remaining intervals
// STEP 3 — CHECK OVERLAP: If they overlap, merge them
// STEP 4 — NO OVERLAP: Add next interval to the list and update current
// STEP 5 — RETURN: Convert list to 2D array

traceSteps.title: 1. Sort Intervals
traceSteps.text: Sort the intervals based on their start times to ensure overlaps are adjacent.

traceSteps.title: 2. Initialize Current
traceSteps.text: Take the first interval and add it to our merged list. Because it's stored by reference, updating it later affects the list.

traceSteps.title: 3. Iterate
traceSteps.text: Loop through the remaining intervals to check for overlap with the current interval.

traceSteps.title: 4. Check Overlap
traceSteps.text: If currentEnd >= nextStart, the intervals overlap. Merge by taking the max end time.

traceSteps.title: 5. No Overlap
traceSteps.text: If they don't overlap, the current interval is complete. Move on to the next interval and add it to the merged list.

sandbox.question: Why do we sort intervals by start time?
options: To ensure overlapping intervals are adjacent to each other.
To reduce the time complexity of merging to O(N).
It's a requirement of the List data structure.

sandbox.question: What is the condition for two intervals (current and next) to overlap?

followUps.q: What if intervals are already sorted?
followUps.a: The time complexity reduces from O(N log N) to O(N) because the sorting step is skipped.

followUps.q: How to handle finding the intersection instead of union?
followUps.a: You check for overlap by `Math.max(start1, start2) <= Math.min(end1, end2)`. The intersection is `[Math.max(start1, start2), Math.min(end1, end2)]`.
